sections.heading: Schedules 4 to
sections.summary: This section specifies that Schedules 4 to 105 of the Act come into effect immediately after the commencement of section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010, but if section 3 does not commence, the provisions do not take effect at all. The section also notes that the table relates only to provisions originally passed by both Houses of Parliament and assented to, excluding any amendments made after assent. The agency may charge a person for accessing information only if the person does not directly download it from a website and the charge specifically covers reproduction or incidental costs incurred by the agency. The agency must publish charge details in the same manner as the information itself. This applies to information access under the Freedom of Information Act 1982, with the Information Commissioner providing guidance on implementation. This section amends exemptions for access to documents under the Freedom of Information Act. It defines edited copies as those with exempt or irrelevant matter deleted, specifies exempt documents as those exempt under Part IV, and establishes conditions for when access to documents expires due to completed review processes.
sections.dtia_summary: Charges for information access must be justified by specific costs and published transparently, which impacts how financial institutions handle public information requests without creating undue barriers for users. This section defines conditions under which access to documents may be withheld due to exemptions or conditional exemptions, impacting how data transfers involving government documents are assessed for public interest compliance. This section requires agencies to create edited copies of documents before disclosure, ensuring only relevant information is shared while maintaining exemp

requirements.text: Schedules 4 to 105 of the Act come into effect immediately after the commencement of section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010.
If section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 does not commence, the provisions do not take effect at all.

The agency may impose a charge on a person for accessing the information only if: (a) the person does not directly access the information by downloading it from the website (or another website); and (b) the charge is to reimburse the agency for specific reproduction costs, or other specific incidental costs, incurred in giving the person access to that particular information.
If there is a charge for accessing the information, the agency must publish details of the charge in the same way as the information is published under this section.
edited copy has the meaning given by section 22 (access to edited copies with exempt or irrelevant matter deleted)
a document that is exempt for the purposes of Part IV (exempt documents) (see section 31B)

run out: all of a person's opportunities for review or appeal in relation to an access grant decision have run out when: (a) the latest time for applying for an internal review or an IC review in relation to the decision has ended, if the person has not applied for either review; or (b) if the person has applied for an internal review in relation to the decision: (i) the internal review is concluded; and (ii) the time for applying for an IC review of the decision on internal review has ended, if the person has not applied for the IC review; or (c) if the person has applied for an IC review in relation to the decision: (i) proceedings in relation to the IC review are concluded; and (ii) the time for applying to the Tribunal for a review in relation to the decision has ended, if the person has not applied for such a review; and (iii) the time for instituting an appeal to the Federal Court in relation to the IC review has ended (with no appeal instituted), or, if an appeal has been instituted, all proceedings in relation to the appeal have been concluded

To avoid doubt, information or matter communicated in the way mentioned in paragraph 33(b) includes information or matter so communicated pursuant to any treaty or formal instrument on the reciprocal protection of classified information between the Government of the Commonwealth, or an authority of the Commonwealth, and: (a) a foreign government or an authority of a foreign government; or (b) an international organisation

This section applies if: (a) a request is made by a person, in accordance with subsection 15(2), to an agency or Minister for access to: (i) a document of the agency; or (ii) an official document of the Minister; and (b) any charge that, under the regulations, is required to be paid before access is given has been paid
The agency or Minister must give the person access to the document in accordance with this Act, subject to this section
The agency or Minister is not required by this Act to give the person access to the document at a particular time if, at that time, the document is an exempt document

The agency or Minister must give the person access to the document if it is conditionally exempt at a particular time unless (in the circumstances) access to the document at that time would, on balance, be contrary to the public interest

Despite subsection (5), the agency or Minister is not required to give access to the document at a particular time if, at that time, the document is both: (a) a conditionally exempt document; and (b) an exempt document: (i) under Division 2 of Part IV (exemptions); or (ii) within the meaning of paragraph (b) or (c) of the definition of exempt document in subsection 4(1)
The agency or Minister must prepare the edited copy as mentioned in paragraph (1)(b)
The agency or Minister must give the applicant access to the edited copy
The agency or Minister must give the applicant notice in writing that the edited copy has been prepared
The agency or Minister must give the applicant notice in writing of the grounds for the deletions
The agency or Minister must give the applicant notice in writing if any matter deleted is exempt matter—that the matter deleted is exempt matter because of a specified provision of this Act
The agency or Minister must give written notice of the decision to both the person concerned and the applicant if access is granted.
Access cannot be given until all review or appeal opportunities for the person concerned have expired and the decision still stands or is confirmed.
The decision to give access is subject to internal review, Information Commissioner review, and Tribunal review.
Subsections (5) and (6) do not apply unless the person concerned makes submissions supporting the exemption contention.

A document is conditionally exempt if its disclosure under this Act would disclose matter (deliberative matter) in the nature of, or relating to, opinion, advice or recommendation obtained, prepared or recorded, or consultation or deliberation that has taken place, in the course of, or for the purposes of, the deliberative processes involved in the functions of an agency or Minister or of the Government of the Commonwealth.
Deliberative matter does not include operational information (see section 8A) or purely factual material.

This section does not apply to reports of scientific or technical experts expressing opinions on scientific or technical matters, reports of prescribed bodies within agencies, or records of final decisions.
Insert: access grant decision has the meaning given by section 53B.
Insert: access refusal decision has the meaning given by section 53A.
Insert: action, if the action is taken by a person or agency, has the same meaning as in the Ombudsman Act 1976.
Insert: affected third party has the meaning given by section 53C.
Insert: authorised person has the meaning given by section 77.
Insert: complainant has the meaning given by subsection 70(1).
If the agency or Minister does not make a decision on the request within the further time allowed
If the agency or Minister does not comply with any condition imposed under subsection (5)
The initial decision period is deemed extended by the time allowed by the Information Commissioner under subsection (4)

The Information Commissioner does not have the power to allow further time under this section in relation to the decision taken to be made under subsection (2) in its operation as affected by subsection (7)
The agency may apply, in writing, to the Information Commissioner for further time to deal with the application.
The Information Commissioner may allow further time considered appropriate by the Information Commissioner for the agency to deal with the application.
If the Information Commissioner allows further time, the Information Commissioner may impose any condition that he or she considers appropriate.
The agency must make a decision on the application within the further time allowed and comply with any condition imposed under subsection (5) to prevent the deemed affirmation from applying.

If the agency does not make a decision within the further time allowed or does not comply with any condition imposed, the deemed affirmation applies as if the initial decision period were extended by the time allowed under subsection (4).
disclose, or enable a person to ascertain, the existence or identity of a confidential source of information, in relation to the enforcement or administration of the law
endanger the life or physical safety of any person
cause damage to the security, defence or international relations of the Commonwealth
The Information Commissioner must hold the hearing in public unless satisfied it is not desirable due to confidential nature of evidence or other reasons.
The Information Commissioner is not bound by the rules of evidence.
The Information Commissioner may hold a part of the hearing in the absence of a review party if necessary to prevent disclosure of confidential information.
The parties to an IC review are the IC review applicant, the principal officer of the agency or Minister, affected third parties (if any), and parties to the proceeding.
A person whose interests are affected may apply in writing to be a review party.
The Information Commissioner may make a person who applies an IC review party by notice in writing.
A review party may apply at any time during an IC review to request a hearing.
The Information Commissioner must notify other review parties of any hearing application.
The Information Commissioner must give all review parties a reasonable opportunity to make submissions on a hearing application.
The Information Commissioner must decide whether to hold a hearing.
A review party may appear in person or be represented by another person.
The agency or Minister concerned has the onus of establishing that a decision is justified or the Information Commissioner should give a decision adverse to the applicant.
The affected third party has the onus of establishing that a refusal decision is justified or the Information Commissioner should give a decision adverse to the requester.
The agency or Minister must use their best endeavours to assist the Information Commissioner in making a decision.
The Information Commissioner may require the agency or Minister to provide an adequate statement of reasons if they believe no statement was provided or it is inadequate.
The agency or Minister must provide an adequate statement of reasons within 28 days or the period specified in the notice.
The Information Commissioner may allow review parties to reach agreement on terms of a decision without completing the IC review.
An agency or Minister may vary an access refusal decision during an IC review if the variation would give access to a document, relieve liability to pay a charge, or require amendment of a record.
The agency or Minister must notify the Information Commissioner in writing of any variation or substitution of an access refusal decision.
The Information Commissioner must treat the IC review application as if it were for the varied or substituted decision.
The Information Commissioner may refer a question of law to the Federal Court of Australia on application or initiative.
The Information Commissioner must not give a decision relevant to the referred question before the Federal Court makes a decision.
The Information Commissioner must not proceed in a manner inconsistent with the Federal Court's opinion on the question.
The Information Commissioner must send all documents related to the IC review and reference to the Federal Court.
The Federal Court must return documents to the Information Commissioner at the conclusion of the proceeding.
After an IC review, the Information Commissioner must make a written decision affirming, varying, or setting aside the IC reviewable decision.
The Information Commissioner may perform the functions and exercise the powers of the person who made the IC reviewable decision.
A decision on an IC review has the same effect as the decision of the agency or Minister.
A decision on an IC review must include a statement of reasons and a statement of rights to apply for review under section 57A.
A decision on an IC review must not include information of the kind referred to in subsection 25(1) or exempt matter.
The Information Commissioner must give a copy of a decision on an IC review to each review party.

A document is prima facie evidence of a decision on an IC review if: (a) the document purports to be a copy of the decision; and (b) the document purports to be certified by, or on behalf of, the Information Commissioner to be a true copy of the decision.
The Information Commissioner must publish a decision on an IC review to members of the public generally.
The Information Commissioner may, by written notice, require a person to appear before the Information Commissioner to answer questions for the purposes of an IC review.
The notice must be in writing.
The notice must state that the person must comply with the notice at a specified time that is not less than 14 days after the time at which the person is given the notice.
The notice must specify the place at which the person must comply with the notice.
A person commits an offence if the person is subject to a requirement specified in a notice under subsection (1), engages in conduct, and the person's conduct breaches the requirement.
Penalty for a contravention of this subsection: 6 months imprisonment.
The Information Commissioner may administer an oath or affirmation to the person and examine the person on oath or affirmation.
The oath or affirmation to be taken or made by a person for the purposes of this section is an oath or affirmation that the answers the person will give will be true.
A person commits an offence if the person is required under this section to be examined on oath or affirmation, engages in conduct, and the person's conduct breaches that requirement.
Information or a document does not cease to be the subject of legal professional privilege merely because it is given, provided, produced or referred to for the purposes of this Part.
This section applies if a person does any of the following in good faith for the purposes of an IC review: gives information, produces a document, answers a question.
Civil proceedings do not lie against a person because the person does any of the matters mentioned in paragraph (1)(a) to (c).
The person is not liable for a penalty under a provision of any law because the person does any of those matters.
The Federal Court of Australia may have regard to the evidence given in the IC review
The Federal Court of Australia may receive further evidence
Subsection (2) does not limit the Federal Court of Australia's power under subsection 56(6) to make an order remitting the case to be heard and decided again by the Information Commissioner
The Administrative Appeals Tribunal Act 1975 applies to proceedings under this Part in the way set out in the following table: Modification of the Administrative Appeals Tribunal Act 1975

The agency or Minister who made the IC reviewable decision is taken to have complied with the obligation under paragraph 37(1)(a) of the Administrative Appeals Tribunal Act 1975 if the agency or Minister gives the AAT the number of copies prescribed by the regulations of the decision under section 55K in relation to which an application has been made to the Tribunal.
Subsection (2) does not limit the powers of the Tribunal under section 38 of the Administrative Appeals Tribunal Act 1975.

sections.source_quote: 7. Schedules 4 to immediately after the commencement of section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010. However, if section 3 of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010 does not commence, the provision(s) do not commence at all.

sections.heading: Note:
sections.summary: The Information Commissioner must notify a respondent agency in writing before starting an investigation. If the Commissioner decides not to investigate or stop an investigation, they must promptly notify the complainant and respondent agency in writing, including the reasons for the decision. This section establishes procedural requirements for initiating and terminating investigations. This section provides immunity from civil liability for individuals who voluntarily provide information, produce documents, or answer questions during good faith investigations conducted by the Information Commissioner. It explicitly states that civil proceedings cannot be brought against such individuals for these actions, and they are not subject to penalties under any law for performing these tasks. The immunity applies regardless of whether the individual is legally required to act under the investigation. This section establishes conditions under which the Ombudsman may recommend amendments to records of opinions. Amendments are permitted only if the opinion was based on a factual mistake or the author was biased, unqualified, or acted improperly. Amendments are prohibited if the record is a c
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires written notification to the respondent agency before commencing an investigation, which is relevant for DTIAs as it defines procedural safeguards for cross-border data investigations involving government agencies. This section ensures that individuals involved in good faith investigations by the Information Commissioner are protected from liability, which may indirectly affect data transfer processes by reducing legal risks during cross-border data investigations. This section does not directly impact data transfer impact assessments as it pertains to record amendments in

requirements.text: Before beginning an investigation, the Information Commissioner must notify the respondent agency in writing.
The Information Commissioner must, as soon as practicable, notify the complainant (if any) and the respondent agency of the decision in writing.
A notice under subsection (3) must state the reasons for the Information Commissioner's decision.
Civil proceedings do not lie against a person because the person does any of the matters mentioned in paragraphs (1)(a) to (c)
The person is not liable for a penalty under a provision of any law because the person does any of those matters

The Ombudsman may recommend amendment to a record of an opinion only if satisfied the opinion was based on a mistake of fact or the author was biased, unqualified, or acted improperly in conducting factual inquiries.

The Ombudsman must not recommend amendment if satisfied the record is a court decision or the amendment involves determining a question the requester is entitled to have resolved internally, via the Information Commissioner, court, or tribunal.
The Ombudsman must consult the Information Commissioner about the complaint with a view to avoid inquiries being conducted into that matter by both the Information Commissioner and the Ombudsman
The Ombudsman may decide not to investigate the action, or not to continue to investigate the action

sections.source_quote: Notice to respondent agency before commencing investigation (1) Before beginning an investigation, the Information Commissioner must notify the respondent agency in writing.

sections.heading: ComLaw Authoritative Act C2012C00866
sections.summary: This section amends multiple legislative instruments by replacing references to the 'Privacy Commissioner' with 'Information Commissioner' across various acts and schedules. It also repeals specific definitions and sections related to the Privacy Commissioner, substituting them with references to the Information Commissioner under the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010. The changes apply to the Privacy Act 1988, Telecommunications Act 1997, Torres Strait Fisheries Act 1984, and other relevant legislation. This section mandates that an agency must ensure it receives a document if the document is created or possessed by a contracted service provider or subcontractor for the Commonwealth contract, the document relates to the performance of the contract (not its entry), and the agency receives a request for access to the document. The requirement applies to agencies handling Commonwealth contracts. This section outlines the process for agencies or Ministers to consult with applicants before refusing access to documents. If the consultation process concludes that a practical refusal reason still exists, the agency or Minister may refuse the request. The consultation period las
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions under which agencies must receive documents from contracted service providers or subcontractors for Commonwealth contracts, relevant to data transfer assessments involving government contracts and third-party data handling. This section establishes a consultation process that must be followed before refusing access to documents, which impacts data transfer impact assessments by requiring agencies to evaluate practical refusal reasons and provide opportunities for request revision before finalizing access decisions. This section is irrelevant to Data Transfer

requirements.text: Omit 'Privacy Commissioner', substitute 'Information Commissioner'
Omit 'written notice', substitute 'legislative instrument'
Repeal the subsections, substitute: When guidelines take effect
Repeal the definition of Privacy Commissioner
Repeal the definition of Commissioner, substitute: Commissioner means the Information Commissioner within the meaning of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010
Repeal the heading, substitute: Part IV—Functions of the Information Commissioner
Omit 'Privacy', substitute nothing
Repeal sections 96, 97 and 99
Omit 'Privacy Commissioner', substitute 'Information Commissioner in relation to matters that relate to the privacy functions (within the meaning of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010)'
Omit 'Privacy Commissioner' (wherever occurring), substitute 'Information Commissioner'
Insert: (1A) The function conferred on the Information Commissioner by subsection (1) is a privacy function for the purposes of the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010
Repeal subsection 309(5) (including the note)

The agency must ensure it receives a document if: (i) the document is created by, or is in the possession of a contracted service provider for the Commonwealth contract; or (ii) a subcontractor for the Commonwealth contract; and (iii) the document relates to the performance of the Commonwealth contract (and not to the entry into that contract); and (iv) the agency receives a request for access to the document.
The agency or Minister must undertake a request consultation process

If, after the request consultation process, the agency or Minister is satisfied that the practical refusal reason still exists, the agency or Minister may refuse to give access to the document in accordance with the request

The agency or Minister must give the applicant a written notice stating the intention to refuse access, the practical refusal reason, the contact person, and details of how to contact the contact person
The consultation period is 14 days after the applicant is given the notice
The agency or Minister must take reasonable steps to assist the applicant to revise the request so that the practical refusal reason no longer exists
The contact person may extend the consultation period with the applicant's agreement
The applicant must, before the end of the consultation period, withdraw the request, make a revised request, or indicate they do not wish to revise the request

Repeal the paragraph, substitute: (a) is: (i) an exempt document under subparagraph 33(a)(i) of the Freedom of Information Act 1982 (documents affecting national security, defence or international relations); or (ii) a conditionally exempt document under section 47C of that Act (deliberative processes) to which access would, on balance, be contrary to the public interest for the purposes of subsection 11A(5) of that Act

Repeal the paragraph, substitute: (a) is: (i) an exempt document under section 47 of the Freedom of Information Act 1982 (trade secrets etc.); or (ii) a conditionally exempt document under section 47G of that Act (business documents) to which access would, on balance, be contrary to the public interest for the purposes of subsection 11A(5) of that Act

Repeal the paragraph, substitute: (a) is: (i) an exempt document under section 47 of the Freedom of Information Act 1982 (trade secrets); or (ii) a conditionally exempt document under section 47G of that Act (business documents) to which access would, on balance, be contrary to the public interest for the purposes of subsection 11A(5) of that Act
The Information Commissioner may take the step in accordance with the provision to implement the decision on or after the commencement day.
The Committee may advise the Information Commissioner in accordance with the functions conferred by section 83 of the Privacy Act 1988 on or after the commencement day.

An investigation for the purposes of a provision of an Act (or an instrument under an Act) that was started by the Privacy Commissioner before the commencement day may be completed by the Information Commissioner in accordance with that provision on or after the commencement day.
The person must provide the evidence, information or document to the Information Commissioner, on the same basis on which it was required to be provided to the Privacy Commissioner.

The person must attend the conference if the conference is: presided over by the Information Commissioner; held on or after the commencement day; and held in accordance with section 46 of the Privacy Act 1988.
The Information Commissioner may determine the application in accordance with the provision in accordance with which the application was made on or after the commencement day.
The Information Commissioner may hold the conference in accordance with section 47 of the Privacy Act 1988 on or after the commencement day.
The Information Commissioner may receive the submissions on or after the commencement day.
The instrument has effect on and after the commencement day as if the reference to the Privacy Commissioner were a reference to the Information Commissioner.
The Information Commissioner must provide the report, as required, in relation to so much of the period as occurs before the commencement day.
The Information Commissioner may finish doing the thing in accordance with the provision on and after the commencement day.
The Information Commissioner may complete the review in accordance with section 18BI of the Privacy Act 1988 on and after the commencement day.
The Information Commissioner may hold the conference in accordance with sections 76 and 77 of the Privacy Act 1988 on or after the commencement day.
The Information Commissioner may make a determination in relation to those matters in accordance with section 79 of the Privacy Act 1988 on or after the commencement day.

All records or documents held, immediately before the commencement day, for the purposes of the performance of the functions, or the exercise of the powers, of the Privacy Commissioner, are, on and after the commencement day, taken to be held for the purposes of the performance of the functions, or the exercise of the powers, of the Information Commissioner.
That section applies to the person in relation to the conduct on and after the commencement day.

A delegation in force under section 99 of the Privacy Act 1988 immediately before the commencement day continues to have effect, subject to the new law, as if it were a delegation under section 25 of the new law on and after the commencement day.
The Governor-General may make regulations prescribing matters required or permitted by this Schedule to be prescribed.
The Governor-General may make regulations prescribing matters necessary or convenient to be prescribed for carrying out or giving effect to this Schedule.

The Governor-General may make regulations prescribing matters of a transitional nature (including prescribing any saving or application provisions) relating to the amendments or repeals made by this Act.

sections.heading: 42  Subsection 135AA(4)
sections.summary: This section amends multiple legislative instruments by replacing references to the 'Privacy Commissioner' with 'Information Commissioner' across various acts and schedules. It also repeals specific definitions and sections related to the Privacy Commissioner, substituting them with references to the Information Commissioner under the Australian Information Commissioner Act 2010. The changes apply to the Privacy Act 1988, Telecommunications Act 1997, Torres Strait Fisheries Act 1984, and other relevant legislation. This section mandates that an agency must ensure it receives a document if the document is created or possessed by a contracted service provider or subcontractor for the Commonwealth contract, the document relates to the performance of the contract (not its entry), and the agency receives a request for access to the document. The requirement applies to agencies handling Commonwealth contracts. This section outlines the process for agencies or Ministers to consult with applicants before refusing access to documents. If the consultation process concludes that a practical refusal reason still exists, the agency or Minister may refuse the request. The consultation period las
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions under which agencies must receive documents from contracted service providers or subcontractors for Commonwealth contracts, relevant to data transfer assessments involving government contracts and third-party data handling. This section establishes a consultation process that must be followed before refusing access to documents, which impacts data transfer impact assessments by requiring agencies to evaluate practical refusal reasons and provide opportunities for request revision before finalizing access decisions. This section is irrelevant to Data Transfer
